narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a coin flip—about a 50% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: it starts with your first-year grades landing you in the bottom decile of your school, which stood out as the strongest signal of struggle ahead, the kind that often foreshadows tougher climbs in law studies. By your third year, you were still in that bottom decile, reinforcing the model's worry that early patterns might stick around. Your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation for many, didn't quite offset those academic hurdles in the way it has for others who've pulled through. Layer on your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 and LSAT score of 40, both pulling in the same direction toward potential failure, painting a picture of consistent challenges in high-stakes testing. Even factors like being a white male studying full-time added their subtle weight, aligning with profiles the model has seen falter before. It's not that any one piece doomed you—it's this combination that made the model cautious, like spotting storm clouds gathering over what could be a promising journey. The good news? You're targeting law school with clear intent, and with targeted support on those early grades and test prep, you could rewrite this story. Hang in there; this is just one snapshot, not the final word on your potential.
